    Gravity and Gravitational Collapse

    Gravitational Collapse

    Type:Foundational concept
    Key scale:Jeans mass/length
    Counteracting forces: Magnetic fields
    Turbulence
    Thermal pressure

    Gravitational collapse is the process by which a self-gravitating body contracts under its own gravity when internal support — from thermal pressure, turbulence, or magnetic fields — is insufficient to maintain equilibrium. In astrophysics, gravitational collapse is the fundamental mechanism driving star formation, from the contraction of molecular cloud cores to the formation of galaxies and galaxy clusters.

    Jeans Instability

    A uniform gas cloud becomes gravitationally unstable when its mass exceeds the Jeans mass M_J — the critical mass above which gravity overwhelms thermal pressure. For a cloud of temperature T and number density n, M_J scales as T^(3/2) n^(-1/2). Equivalently, collapse occurs on scales larger than the Jeans length λ_J.

    In practice, molecular clouds are not uniform or purely thermally supported. Turbulence provides additional support on large scales (but can also compress gas locally to trigger collapse), while magnetic fields provide non-thermal support quantified by the mass-to-flux ratio.

    Magnetic Support and the Critical Mass-to-Flux Ratio

    A magnetized cloud is subcritical when the mass-to-flux ratio λ < 1 — magnetic pressure prevents collapse. It is supercritical when λ > 1 and gravity dominates. Clouds near λ ~ 1 are transcritical, as observed in the IRDC G351.77-0.53 where λ ~ 0.9 ± 0.6[1].

    In subcritical clouds, collapse can still proceed via ambipolar diffusion — the slow drift of neutral gas through the magnetically coupled ion-electron fluid[2][3]. As mass accumulates faster than flux can escape, the core transitions to supercritical and undergoes dynamic collapse.

    Signatures of Collapse

    • Hourglass B-field morphology — gravitational contraction drags initially uniform field lines inward, creating a pinched geometry[1][3]
    • Infall velocity gradients — redshifted/blueshifted asymmetries in optically thick lines
    • Increasing mass-to-flux ratio — λ increases from cloud envelope to dense core as gravity wins[1]
    • Energy equipartition — gravitational, magnetic, and kinetic energies become comparable in actively collapsing regions[1]

    See Also

    • Star formation — the outcome of cloud collapse
    • Mass-to-flux ratio — magnetic criticality diagnostic
    • Magnetic fields — support against collapse
    • Turbulence — both supporting and triggering collapse
    • Hourglass B-field morphology — collapse signature
    • Jeans equation

    References
    1. Jadhav, O. R., et al. (2025). "Hourglass-Shaped Magnetic Field toward IRDC G351.77-0.53." arXiv:2603.16425
    2. Mouschovias, T. Ch. (1976). "Magnetic Field Support Against Gravitational Collapse." ApJ, 207, 141. DOI:10.1086/154835
    3. Nakamura, F. & Li, Z.-Y. (2008). "Magnetically Regulated Star Formation." ApJ, 687, 354. DOI:10.1086/591641
